chunk_summary: This section addresses food selectivity in neurodevelopmental disorders, emphasizing sensory sensitivities, nutritional deficiencies, and feeding strategies, including responsive routines and multidisciplinary care for severe cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of sensory sensitivities and rigid routines in food selectivity among children with neurodevelopmental disorders.
Identify common nutritional deficiencies (e.g., iron, calcium, vitamin D, fiber) associated with restrictive eating patterns.
Learn strategies for dietary assessment, including documenting accepted foods, triggers, and feeding observations.
Recognize when to escalate care to a multidisciplinary team for severe feeding challenges or ARFID.
key_facts: Food selectivity in neurodevelopmental disorders, such as ASD, is often linked to sensory sensitivities (e.g., texture, smell, color) and rigid routines.
Common nutritional risks include iron deficiency, low calcium and vitamin D intake, inadequate fiber, and low vitamin B12 in restrictive diets.
Dietary assessment should document accepted foods, refused textures, and triggers like brand changes or new utensils.
Responsive feeding routines and graded exposure (neutral presentations without pressure) are recommended over coercive feeding.
ARFID diagnosis should be considered when restriction leads to weight loss, nutritional deficiency, or psychosocial impairment, using DSM-5 criteria.
Multidisciplinary care (developmental pediatrics, dietitian, OT/SLP) is essential for severe cases or minimal progress.
reference_quote: "Consider ARFID when restriction is severe and causes weight loss, nutritional deficiency, dependence on supplements, or psychosocial impairment; apply DSM-5 constructs when mental health support is available." (Source: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, DSM-5)
document_key_topics: Responsive feeding strategies and graded exposure
Multidisciplinary care for severe feeding challenges
